From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Representative rugby league team made up of Māori players from New Zealand

Not to be confused with [Māori All Blacks](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_All_Blacks "Māori All Blacks").

For the women's team, see [New Zealand Māori women's rugby league team](/wiki/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_women%27s_rugby_league_team "New Zealand Māori women's rugby league team").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_rugby_league_team "Special:EditPage/New Zealand Māori rugby league team") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["New Zealand Māori rugby league team"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22New+Zealand+M%C4%81ori+rugby+league+team%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

New Zealand Māori

| Team information | |
| --- | --- |
| Head coach | [Adam Blair](/wiki/Adam_Blair "Adam Blair") |
| Captain | [James Fisher-Harris](/wiki/James_Fisher-Harris "James Fisher-Harris") |
| Team results | |
| **First international** | |
| [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") 24–14 Māori  ([Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia; 1908) | |
| **Biggest win** | |
| Māori 64–4 [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau_national_rugby_league_team "Tokelau national rugby league team")  (Ericsson Stadium No.2, [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"); 2006) | |
| **Biggest defeat** | |
| Māori 0–29 [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team")  ([Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand; 20 July 1910) | |
| **[World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup")** | |

The **New Zealand Māori rugby league team** is a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") representative side made up of [New Zealand Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") players.

With some controversy, the team participated in the [2000 World Cup](/wiki/2000_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2000 Rugby League World Cup") as **Aotearoa Māori**.[[1]](#cite_note-wilson-maori26-10-00-1) The [Super League International Board](/wiki/Super_League_International_Board "Super League International Board") had agreed to give a place in their World Cup to the New Zealand Māori team as they attempted to gain allies during the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war").[[1]](#cite_note-wilson-maori26-10-00-1) Despite that World Cup not taking place, the [Rugby League International Federation](/wiki/Rugby_League_International_Federation "Rugby League International Federation") repeated the offer for the [2000 World Cup](/wiki/2000_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2000 Rugby League World Cup") when it replaced the Super League International Board following the end of the dispute.[[1]](#cite_note-wilson-maori26-10-00-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Maori_team_2008_RLWC.jpg/250px-Maori_team_2008_RLWC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maori_team_2008_RLWC.jpg)

New Zealand Maori pre–match huddle before their clash with the [Indigenous Dreamtime team](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_rugby_league_team "Australian Aboriginal rugby league team") before the start of the [2008 World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup")

A New Zealand Māori team first toured overseas in 1908 when they visited Australia. This tour was a success, and was followed by another tour to Australia in 1909 and to Great Britain in 1910.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Wairangi_Koopu_2008.jpg/250px-Wairangi_Koopu_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wairangi_Koopu_2008.jpg)

Wairangi Koopu takes on the Indigenous line

The first game of international rugby league on New Zealand soil was between the Māori and the [touring Great Britain Lions of 1910](/wiki/1910_Great_Britain_Lions_tour_of_Australia_and_New_Zealand "1910 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

A separate body, the Māori Rugby League Board of Control, was formed in 1934 to administer the game in Māori communities.[[3]](#cite_note-leberman-2005-p69-3) This governing body was later renamed the Aotearoa Māori Rugby League and in 1992 it was registered as an incorporated society.[[3]](#cite_note-leberman-2005-p69-3)

The Māori have had a wonderful record of beating international touring teams over the years. In 1983 they visited Britain and a side containing future Kiwi stars like [Hugh McGahan](/wiki/Hugh_McGahan "Hugh McGahan"), [Dean Bell](/wiki/Dean_Bell "Dean Bell") and [Clayton Friend](/wiki/Clayton_Friend "Clayton Friend") proved too strong for the amateur opposition they played. For many years, the Māori have competed in the Pacific Cup alongside other teams with a strong presence of New Zealand–based players—Samoa, Tonga and the Cook Islands, so they thought it was right they should have the opportunity to follow these teams to the World Cup. The invitation to the Māori to take part in the 2000 World Cup came about as a result of promises made to them by the defunct [Super League International Board](/wiki/Super_League_International_Board "Super League International Board") at the height of the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war") that tore the game apart in the southern hemisphere.

The Māori team has participated in the [Pacific Cup](/wiki/Pacific_Cup "Pacific Cup") (since [1974](/wiki/1974_Pacific_Cup "1974 Pacific Cup")), Super League's [1997 Oceania Cup](/wiki/1997_Oceania_Cup "1997 Oceania Cup"), Papua New Guinea 50th Anniversary (1998), [2000 World Cup](/wiki/2000_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2000 Rugby League World Cup"), World Sevens Qualification (2003) and [Pacific Rim](/wiki/2004_Pacific_Cup "2004 Pacific Cup") (2004) competitions.[[3]](#cite_note-leberman-2005-p69-3)

The Māori competed against [Indigenous Dreamtime team](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_rugby_league_team "Australian Aboriginal rugby league team") on 26 October 2008 as the curtain raiser to the first match of the [2008 World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Māori team lost 34–26.

In 2010, the Māori team played England at Mt. Smart Stadium in Auckland before the 2010 [Rugby League Four Nations](/wiki/Rugby_League_Four_Nations "Rugby League Four Nations") in New Zealand. After trailing 18–0 at halftime, the Māori came back to draw the match at 18–all.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Aboriginal_war_dance_meets_Maori_haka_2008_RLWC.jpg/250px-Aboriginal_war_dance_meets_Maori_haka_2008_RLWC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aboriginal_war_dance_meets_Maori_haka_2008_RLWC.jpg)

Maori Haka Meets Indigenous War Cry

In October 2013, the side faced the touring [Murri Rugby League Team](/wiki/Murri_Rugby_League_Team "Murri Rugby League Team") in a two–game series. The Māori side, featuring NRL players [Charlie Gubb](/wiki/Charlie_Gubb "Charlie Gubb"), [Sam Rapira](/wiki/Sam_Rapira "Sam Rapira") and [Bodene Thompson](/wiki/Bodene_Thompson "Bodene Thompson"), won the first game 48–18 at Davies Park, [Huntly](/wiki/Huntly,_New_Zealand "Huntly, New Zealand"). The second game was played at Puketawhero Park, [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua,_New_Zealand "Rotorua, New Zealand") and was won by the Māori side, 32–16.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In October 2014, the team travelled to Australia to play against the Queensland Māori team at Owen Park, [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland") and the [Murri Rugby League Team](/wiki/Murri_Rugby_League_Team "Murri Rugby League Team") at BMD Kougari Oval, [Wynnum](/wiki/Wynnum,_Queensland "Wynnum, Queensland").

In 2018 they took part in the NRL Festival of Indigenous Rugby League held in Redfern Sydney against the First Nation Goannas, they were beaten 22–16 in a thrilling finish.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
